PH1.template: Here's what every business owner paying suppliers in [region] needs to know about [topic].
PH1.text: Here's what every business owner paying suppliers in China needs to know about wire conversions.

If you pay Chinese suppliers in CNY, here is something most banks will not tell you. Your CNY can quietly get swapped to USD up to three times on the way over, and your supplier ends up short. In this video we walk through exactly where it happens.

PH2.text: After five years of CNY wires, business owners realise one thing: the spread compounds.

PH2.text: This is 180,000 dollars of currency hops nobody told you existed.

PH2.template: In this video: how [small thing] quietly adds up over [time].
PH2.text: In this video: how a small wire fee quietly adds up over five years.

Every time you wire CNY to a Chinese factory, your money may get swapped between currencies three times, each with a hidden spread. Stretch that over five years of orders and it can quietly drain six figures from your business. Here is the math.

PH3.template: Here's where [amount] can quietly disappear when you [action].
PH3.text: Here's where 1,500 dollars can quietly disappear when you wire money to China.

When you send a 50K CNY wire to China, your supplier can end up receiving the equivalent of 47K and nobody can tell you where the 3K went. That is because three different banks each take a quiet slice on the way. Here is the trail.

PH4.template: What (insert title) says vs what they mean.
PH4.text: What a wire form says vs what its underlying rails actually carry.

PH4.template: In this video: what actually happens when you [action].
PH4.text: In this video: what actually happens when you wire CNY to a supplier in China.

You think your bank sends CNY straight to your Chinese supplier. It does not. It runs on USD rails, so your money is swapped to USD, routed through a middle bank, then swapped back to CNY at the other end. Here is why each hop costs you.

PH5.template: Here's what every [audience] needs to know about [topic].
PH5.text: Here's what every business owner wiring money to China needs to know about hidden spreads.

If you wire money to China, there are three points where your CNY can quietly become USD and back again, each with a small currency spread taken from your margin. Stack those and your supplier lands short, or worse, sitting on USD. Here is what to watch for.

PG1.text: They said you sent CNY. You didn't. The bank routed USD.

PG1.text: What your bank says vs what three correspondent banks actually do.

PG1.text: After years of wires, business owners realise one thing: banks don't carry CNY.

PG1.template: Here's what really happens when you [action].
PG1.text: Here's what really happens when you send a CNY wire from your bank.

Your bank app says CNY on the screen, but behind the scenes most banks swap that into USD before it leaves, route it through a middle bank, then swap it back at your supplier's end. Three swaps. Three spreads. Here is what really moves.

PG2.template: This is (insert large number) of (insert item).
PG2.text: This is 30,000 dollars a year quietly hopping through three banks.

PG2.template: In this video: why most banks don't actually [action], even when you ask them to.
PG2.text: In this video: why most banks don't actually send CNY, even when you ask them to.

Most banks do not actually send CNY when you ask them to, even though your screen says so. They send USD and let a swap happen down the line, with about 1% taken at each of three banks. On regular supplier payments, that drains thousands a year from your margin.

PG3.text: Stop using a USD-rail bank to send CNY to suppliers.

PG3.text: If your bank carries USD rails, don't ask it to send CNY.

PG3.text: Here's exactly how to lock in real CNY rails for every supplier payment.

PG3.template: What (insert title) says vs what they mean.
PG3.text: What your bank's wire form says vs what its rails actually carry.

PG3.template: Here's why your [tool] might be the wrong tool for [task].
PG3.text: Here's why your bank might be the wrong tool for paying Chinese suppliers.

Banks move international money on pipes called rails, and most banks have USD rails, not CNY rails. So when you ask your bank to send CNY to China, it cannot do it end to end. Here is why that costs you a spread at every hop.

PG4.text: Here are the silent FX moves banks pull on every China wire. Worth knowing.

PG4.template: What (insert title) says vs what they mean.
PG4.text: What a wire statement shows vs what three banks really did to your money.

PG4.template: In this video: where the FX conversion really happens on [thing].
PG4.text: In this video: where the FX conversion really happens on your China wire.

You probably think your bank does the currency swap once at the start of a China wire. It does not. Three different banks can each book their own rate and take their own spread, and you will never see it on your statement. Here is where it really happens.

PG5.template: Here's why no one can tell you where [thing happened].
PG5.text: Here's why no one can tell you where your CNY became USD.

When a China wire goes wrong, every bank in the chain points at the other and nobody can tell you where the swap happened. That is because there are three possible places it can happen, all hidden from your statement. Here is why the phone-tag never ends.
